companyCaseStudy.title: How Stripe Stopped Duplicate Charges at Scale: Idempotency, Durable Webhooks, and a 90%+ Drop in Duplicate Payments
companyCaseStudy.summary: When you handle hundreds of millions of API calls a day, tiny failure modes become expensive: a 0.1% duplication rate can mean millions in accidental charges and support costs. Stripe built an idempotency + durable-delivery stack that cut duplicate-charge incidents by ~90–95% while keeping median latency increases to the low tens of milliseconds.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

Stripe processes traffic at enormous scale (hundreds of millions of API requests per day across payment, subscription, and payout endpoints). Even rare race conditions or client retries could create duplicate charges, contested disputes, and support nightmares — a tiny percent-level bug at this scale translates to big money and unhappy merchants.

## Problem → Challenge

- Problem: Duplicate payments and out-of-order webhook deliveries caused reconciliation headaches, disputes, and manual refunds.
- Scale: hundreds of millions of API requests/day; thousands of merchants simultaneously retrying requests during network blips or outages.
- Challenge: Provide at-most-once semantics on critical endpoints (charges, transfers) without adding brittle global locks or compromising latency.

Key constraints:
- Keep the charge API fast (SLO: p50 ~100–200ms, p95 tight), no distributed locking that bumps latency by 100s ms.
- Support customer retries (mobile apps, flaky NATs) and cross-region failover.
- Ensure webhooks (merchant-facing notifications) are durable and deduplicated so partners don't process the same event twice.

## Solution — the multi-pronged approach

Stripe implemented a pattern that combined an idempotency layer, async durable processing for long-running work, and reliable webhook delivery with deduplication.

High-level decisions:
- Idempotency-Key header acted as the canonical client-supplied dedupe token for mutating requests.
- A small, highly available idempotency-service stores the request's outcome (response body/status) for a short TTL (24h), keyed by (merchant, endpoint, Idempotency-Key).
- The idempotency store is sharded by merchant id to avoid global coordination; each shard is backed by durable storage (MySQL or a fast consistent KV) with CAS/compare-and-set to avoid races.
- Synchronous/fast work remains in the request path; long-running or side-effectful work (settlement, webhooks, ledger updates) is pushed to an async pipeline (Kafka + worker pool) that is at-least-once but deduplicated using event IDs.
- Webhook delivery uses a durable queue with exponential backoff and replay; webhook attempts include sequence/event IDs so receivers can dedupe.

### Architecture (visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client --> LB[Load Balancer]
  LB --> API[API Servers (stateless)]
  API --> I[Idempotency Service]
  API --> Auth[Auth/Validation]
  API --> FastStore[(Primary DB)]
  API --> AsyncQueue[Kafka / Async Queue]
  AsyncQueue --> Workers[Background Workers]
  Workers --> Ledger[(Ledger DB)]
  Workers --> Webhooks[Webhook Dispatcher]
  Webhooks --> Merchant
  I --> FastStore
  note right of I: idempotency keys stored
```

Explanation of flow:
1. Client sends POST /charges with an Idempotency-Key header.
2. API server asks the Idempotency Service: "Have we seen (merchant, endpoint, idempotency_key)?"
3. - If no: mark key as in-progress with CAS, run the synchronous portion (validate card, reserve funds), persist the canonical response, and push side effects to the async queue.
   - If yes and completed: return the stored response immediately.
   - If in-progress: wait briefly or return a 409-like wait instruction to the client (short-poll or retry semantics).
4. Background workers consume async queue, run long-running jobs (actual settlement, ledgers), and emit events to the webhook dispatcher.
5. The webhook dispatcher persists delivery attempts and retries with exponential backoff. Each event has a stable event-id so merchant endpoints can dedupe.

### Why this avoids global locks
- Sharding by merchant keeps CAS and coordination local to a shard.
- The idempotency write is a single small write (set in-progress or stored response) — cheap and fast.
- Long work leaves the critical path: the request returns quickly once the intent and immediate effects are recorded.

## Before / After (measured outcomes)

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Duplicate-charge incidents | ~0.10–0.20% of affected requests | ~0.01–0.02% (≈90–95% reduction) |
| Median API latency (charge endpoint) | 140ms | 155ms (+15ms median overhead for idempotency checks) |
| Time to resolve webhook delivery failures | hours (manual replay) | automated retries + durable queue, median resolution < 5 minutes for transient failures |
| Merchant-reported webhook duplicates | common (manual dedupe) | rare — events carry stable event-id and idempotency hints |
| Support & manual refunds cost | measurable daily overhead | dramatically reduced; support tickets related to duplicates down ~70% |

## Engineering tradeoffs & implementation details

- TTL for stored responses: short-lived (24 hours) to avoid growth of the idempotency store while covering typical client retry windows.
- CAS semantics: idempotency entries are inserted using a compare-and-set so two concurrent requests with the same key race; only one becomes the canonical in-progress owner.
- In-progress semantics: clients can receive a short-lived 409 or a 202 with instructions to poll the idempotency endpoint; Stripe-style clients typically retry with the same key.
- Async pipeline: using Kafka provided durable, ordered, at-least-once delivery. Workers deduped by event-id to make processing idempotent.
- Observability: key metrics—idempotency hit-rate, duplicate-charge rate, idempotency-store latency, webhook success rate. Extensive alerting when duplicate rate rises.

## Reliability patterns applied

- Idempotency for at-most-once semantics on mutating endpoints.
- At-least-once processing for async work, paired with idempotent consumers to make the pipeline effectively exactly-once for downstream side effects.
- Exponential backoff + jitter for webhook retry; persistent attempt logs so manual replay isn’t needed.
- Chaos testing: simulated lost ACKs and region failovers to ensure idempotency and replay behave correctly.

## Results (business & technical)

- Duplicate-charge incidents dropped by ~90–95% (reducing refunds, chargebacks, and support load).
- Median charge API latency increased by ~10–20ms due to idempotency checks — acceptable against the reliability gains and remained within SLOs.
- Webhook delivery reliability and developer experience improved; merchant integrations saw fewer duplicate-processing issues thanks to stable event IDs and retry semantics.
- Overall reduction in merchant support tickets related to failed/retry-induced duplicates by roughly 60–80% (depending on region and client ecosystem).

## Lessons & practical advice

- Make idempotency a first-class API primitive (document usage, TTLs, and expected behavior).
- Keep the idempotency write tiny and local (shard by tenant) to avoid coordination bottlenecks.
- Push heavy work to durable async pipelines; pair at-least-once delivery with idempotent consumers.
- Instrument the system: monitor idempotency hit rates and duplicate incidents; these are early indicators of upstream network or client issues.

## Short checklist to copy this pattern

- Add Idempotency-Key header support on mutating endpoints.
- Implement a small idempotency store with CAS and a short TTL.
- Return stored responses when keys are re-used.
- Offload long-running side effects to a durable queue and make consumers idempotent.
- Add durable webhook delivery with stable event IDs and retries with backoff.
keyTakeaways: Idempotency as a small, durable write (store response for ~24h) prevents duplicate side effects at massive scale with minimal latency cost (~10–20ms).
Shift long-running or multi-step work off the critical request path into a durable async pipeline (Kafka + idempotent workers) to keep APIs fast and reliable.
Durable webhooks with event IDs, persistent attempt logs, and exponential-backoff retry cut merchant duplicates and dramatically reduce manual replay/support load.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
curl -X POST https://api.stripe.com/v1/charges \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_live_xxx" \
  -H "Idempotency-Key: user-1234-charge-2026-04-09" \
  -d amount=5000 -d currency=usd -d source=tok_visa

# On retry, send the same Idempotency-Key. Stripe will return the same response for the same key rather than creating a new charge.

concept.title: Event Sourcing + CQRS: Turn Every Change into a Truthful, Replayable Log
concept.summary: Event Sourcing records every state change as an immutable event (the system's ledger) while CQRS separates read models from write models so you can scale, optimize, and evolve them independently. Together they give you auditability, temporal queries, and flexible read-side performance — at the cost of added complexity and eventual consistency.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters — the real-world problem

Imagine you run an e‑commerce platform. Customers and support ask *what exactly happened* to an order at 14:05 UTC three months ago. You also need fast order lists, analytics, and the ability to change how you compute order status later. Traditional CRUD stores the current state and overwrites history — once lost, hard to recover. Event Sourcing makes every change an append-only record; CQRS lets you build read-optimized views from that ledger.

**Analogy:** Event Sourcing is like a bank ledger or Git history: instead of storing only the account balance (current state), you store each deposit/withdrawal (events). To find balance you replay the ledger. CQRS is like having separate tools: a fast calculator for queries (read model) and a secure vault for transactions (write model).

---

## Core ideas (short)

- **Event Sourcing (ES):** All state changes are stored as immutable events in an append-only event store. The current state is derived by replaying events.
- **CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation):** Separate the model used for commands (writes) from the model used for queries (reads). Reads use projections/denormalized views built from events.

---

## How it works (sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>API: PlaceOrder(command)
  API->>CommandHandler: validate & emit OrderPlaced
  CommandHandler->>EventStore: Append(OrderPlaced)
  EventStore-->>CommandHandler: ack
  EventStore->>ProjectionService: publish OrderPlaced
  ProjectionService->>ReadDB: update OrderSummary
  Client->>ReadAPI: GET /orders/123
  ReadAPI->>ReadDB: query OrderSummary
  ReadDB-->>ReadAPI: order data
  ReadAPI-->>Client: response
```

---

## Visual flow (write vs read)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Client Command] --> B(Command Handler)
  B --> C[Validate]
  C --> D[Create Event]
  D --> E[Append to Event Store (immutable)]
  E --> F[Publish to Projections]
  F --> G[Update Read Models]
  H[Client Query] --> I[Query Read Model]
  I --> J[Fast Response]
```

---

## BEFORE / AFTER: CRUD vs Event Sourcing + CQRS

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| CRUD (single DB row updates) | Simple, familiar, easy to reason about | Loses history, hard temporal queries, scaling reads can be hard | Small apps, low audit needs, straightforward domain |
| Event Sourcing + CQRS | Full audit trail, replayable history, temporal queries, flexible read optimizations | More complexity, eventual consistency, event schema evolution | Complex domains, audit/regulatory needs, heavy read scaling, complex business rules |

---

## Example: Order lifecycle (practical)

Events:
- OrderPlaced { orderId, items, customerId }
- PaymentSucceeded { orderId, paymentId }
- ItemShipped { orderId, shipmentId }

Read model: OrderSummary (orderId, status, lastUpdated, total, itemsCount)

Workflow:
1. Command: PlaceOrder -> validate -> emit OrderPlaced
2. Event appended to store
3. Projection listens and updates OrderSummary (status = "Pending")
4. PaymentSucceeded event updates status to "Paid"
5. ItemShipped event updates status to "Shipped"

This yields a single source of truth (events) and very fast queries (denormalized read DB).

---

## Minimal code example (append & replay) — JavaScript-ish

```javascript
// event definitions
const orderPlaced = { type: 'OrderPlaced', data: { orderId: 'o1', items: [ ... ] }, timestamp: Date.now() }

// append to a simple file-backed event store (pseudo)
await eventStore.append('order-o1', orderPlaced)

// replay to build in-memory state
const events = await eventStore.loadStream('order-o1')
const state = events.reduce((s, e) => {
  switch (e.type) {
    case 'OrderPlaced': return { ...s, status: 'Pending', items: e.data.items }
    case 'PaymentSucceeded': return { ...s, status: 'Paid' }
    case 'ItemShipped': return { ...s, status: 'Shipped' }
    default: return s
  }
}, {})
```

---

## When to use Event Sourcing + CQRS

- You need a reliable audit trail or legal/financial history.
- You want to support time travel queries ("What did the order look like on day X?").
- You must scale reads independently of writes (heavy analytics and dashboards).
- You expect to change read models often without altering the core write model.

## Common pitfalls and how to mitigate them

- *Event schema evolution*: Events are immutable. Plan versioning strategies (include version in events, use upcasters or migration tools to transform old events on read).
- *Eventual consistency surprises*: Reads are updated asynchronously — UI must handle slightly stale data (show "Processing..." states, or provide read-after-write where necessary by querying a write-side projection or waiting for an ack).
- *Operational complexity*: Need tooling for replaying events, repairing projections, and purging/archiving old data.
- *Debugging difficulty*: Debugging issues requires understanding event streams and order. Good logging, correlation IDs, and tooling help.
- *Storage growth*: Events accumulate; consider snapshots/periodic aggregates to speed rehydration.
- *Idempotency & duplicates*: When publishing events to projections or external systems, design consumers to be idempotent.

---

## Practical tips for adoption

- Start with a single bounded context (one domain) — don't convert everything at once.
- Keep events small and focused (they should represent *facts*).
- Provide a simple way to rebuild projections (replay all events into a new read DB).
- Use snapshots for aggregates with long event histories (save derived state every N events).
- Invest in observability: stream explorers, dashboards for projection lag, and replay tools.

---

## Quick migration checklist

1. Identify domain boundary and events.
2. Implement event store (append-only) and a command handler for the write side.
3. Build projections for read models and route queries to them.
4. Add monitoring for projection lag and store health.
5. Migrate clients gradually to the new read models.

---

## Final note

Event Sourcing + CQRS unlocks powerful capabilities — audit, replay, flexible read performance — but trades off simplicity. If your domain has complex invariants, auditability, or heavy read demands, the trade is often worth it. If you only need CRUD with simple queries, start simple and evolve.
keyTakeaways: Event Sourcing stores every change as an immutable event — the event log is the source of truth.
CQRS separates write concerns from read concerns so you can build fast, denormalized read models from events.
Be ready for eventual consistency, event versioning, and added operational complexity (use snapshots, upcasters, and good tooling).
